serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Ginger and Spring Onion Sliced Fish is approximately 250 g, representing a typical restaurant or home-cooked portion served alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, tilapia, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with scallion and ginger component data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for lean cooked white fish (tilapia/snakehead proxy), adjusted for the addition of ginger, spring onion, cornstarch velveting, soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, and sesame oil. Saturated fat estimated from USDA cooking-oil and fish fat profiles. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available at composite dish level; set to -1. Cholesterol estimated from USDA tilapia data. Water content not available at composite dish level; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish and starch dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate cooked fish with minimal cornstarch
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ginger and Spring Onion Sliced Fish contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, derived primarily from a small amount of cornstarch used in velveting and trace amounts from ginger and spring onion. No direct GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this dish. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the analogous profile of lean cooked fish with minimal starch coating; the negligible carbohydrate content means the glycemic load per 250 g serving is approximately 0.9 g, which is clinically insignificant. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (15 × 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 / 100 = 0.94, rounded to 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: At 280 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 700 mg per standard 250 g serving), individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and avoid adding extra salt; the dish's high potassium content (240 mg/100 g) partially offsets sodium impact and supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein, very low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and 14.5 g protein, this dish has a negligible glycemic impact and is an excellent high-protein main dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, supporting satiety and blood glucose stability when paired with a controlled carbohydrate side.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein, low saturated fat, anti-inflammatory ginger
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean white fish provides heart-healthy protein with low saturated fat (approximately 1.1 g/100 g); ginger has documented anti-inflammatory properties; individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake from soy sauce and request minimal added oil during preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ginger and fish omega-3 content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger is a well-established anti-inflammatory agent and white fish provides omega-3 fatty acids; this dish is generally well-suited for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as part of an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginger and Spring Onion Sliced Fish (姜葱鱼片, Jiāngcōng yúpiàn) is a classic Cantonese home-style and restaurant dish originating from Guangdong Province, China. It features delicate slices of lean white fish — commonly snakehead (生鱼), grouper (石斑鱼), or tilapia (罗非鱼) — that are first 'velveted' by marinating in a mixture of cornstarch and egg white to achieve a silky, tender texture before being quickly stir-fried in a hot wok. The dish is seasoned with fresh ginger julienne, spring onions (scallions), light soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, and a finishing drizzle of sesame oil. The result is a clean, aromatic, lightly sauced dish with a delicate savory-sweet flavor profile and a silky-smooth fish texture. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (approximately 14.5 g per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (2.5 g per 100 g), and moderate in fat (5.2 g per 100 g), making it suitable for weight management, blood glucose control, and cardiovascular health. It provides meaningful amounts of vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg/100 g), selenium (18.5 mcg/100 g), phosphorus (145 mg/100 g), and potassium (240 mg/100 g). The sodium content (280 mg/100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. A standard serving is approximately 250 g, typically served alongside steamed jasmine rice as part of a multi-dish Cantonese meal. The dish is suitable for lunch and dinner and is a staple of Cantonese home cooking and casual restaurant menus across China, Hong Kong, and Chinese diaspora communities in Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for lean cooked white fish (closest approved-source equivalent) with adjustments for the composite dish ingredients including ginger, spring onion, cornstarch velveting, soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, and sesame oil. No exact composite dish entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Cantonese stir-fried fish dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite cooked dish is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible for this preparation using vegetable cooking oil and no hydrogenated fats. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100 g) estimated from USDA tilapia fat profile and typical vegetable oil contribution. Cholesterol (45 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA cooked tilapia data. Glycemic index is estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous lean cooked fish with minimal cornstarch starch coating per University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate methodology; the dish contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and the glycemic load per 250 g serving is approximately 0.9, which is clinically negligible. The dish is not strictly low-sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical 250 g entries); only one default serving of 250 g is retained. FoodOn mapped to broad 'fish dish' class; no species-specific or preparation-specific FoodOn term exists for this Cantonese dish. FoodEx2 A00LC used as closest defensible category for cooked fish dish. Iteration 3 fixes: (1) glycemic_index changed from null to numeric value 15 to satisfy q4 numeric requirement; gi_category updated to 'low'; glycemic_load_per_serving updated to 0.9; gi_source updated to analogous_estimate with full rationale. (2) aliases_by_language chinese array corrected to include '薑蔥魚片' (traditional form) and all entries verified verbatim present in flat aliases array to satisfy q8.
